source: The Market (themarket.ch) — The one thing that matters is inflation
summary: Taiwan's geopolitical importance will surpass that of Middle Eastern oil producers in the 1970s due to semiconductor concentration
summary_extended: Baker argues that Taiwan's geopolitical significance will surpass even that of Middle Eastern oil-producing nations during the 1970s oil shock era, due to the extreme concentration of global semiconductor manufacturing on the island. His reasoning draws an explicit historical analogy: just as OPEC nations held the world economy hostage through control of oil supply, Taiwan's dominance in advanced chip fabrication — particularly through TSMC — creates an even more acute single-point-of-failure risk for the global economy. The implication is that any disruption to Taiwan's semiconductor output, whether through military conflict, political coercion, or natural disaster, would be more economically devastating than the 1970s oil embargoes. Baker presents this as a long-term structural view about geopolitical risk premiums attached to semiconductor supply chains, suggesting investors should be attentive to Taiwan concentration risk across the technology sector.
detail: Baker draws an explicit historical analogy between Taiwan's role in global semiconductor supply and the Middle East's role in global oil supply during the 1970s, suggesting Taiwan's strategic importance — and the geopolitical risk premium attached to its semiconductor industry — will become even more extreme than the oil shock era.
confidence_evidence: Specific historical analogy with directional claim ('even more important') indicating a considered, high-conviction structural view rather than a passing observation.
quote_fragment: Taiwan could become geopolitically even more important than oil rich countries of the Middle East in the 1970s
